old_claim.note: Enterprise Resource Planning provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No direct ERP dependency remains because warehouse barcode automation is scoped to barcode-enabled identification and warehouse workflow execution, not to enterprise-wide ERP suites.

source_shape.source_locator: The ERP review dates the ERP term to Gartner in 1990 as a new generation of MRP systems, while this warehouse node is anchored to barcode and distribution-center automation antecedents.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: ERP is a later integrated enterprise-software category, not the direct automatic-identification mechanism for warehouse barcode automation.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Keeping ERP as an enabling prerequisite would make the warehouse barcode node depend on a broad later enterprise-planning suite instead of the directly sourced barcode and inventory-visibility mechanisms.

ontology_before: Warehouse Barcode Automation depended directly on ERP, conflating enterprise planning suites with warehouse-floor barcode identification and inventory workflows.
ontology_after: Warehouse Barcode Automation depends on barcodes and supply-chain management software; ERP remains adjacent enterprise integration context rather than a direct prerequisite.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: enterprise_resource_planning is no longer a direct prerequisite.
would_reject_if: The node were rescoped to ERP-integrated warehouse modules only.
A stronger source showed ERP suites were required for the represented first warehouse barcode automation systems.
A separate ERP warehouse-module node replaced this broader barcode-automation node.
short_reason: ERP is broad integration context, not the barcode automation mechanism.
